1.synthesis: Falling on his face before the glory, Ezekiel hears, “Son of man, stand on your feet, and I will speak with you” (v.1); the Spirit enters him and sets him on his feet (v.2). God sends him to “a rebellious house” — impudent and stubborn — “whether they hear or refuse to hear… they will know that a prophet has been among them” (vv.3–5). The integrative thread is the commissioning of the prophet to a rebellious people regardless of response: God raises the prostrate prophet by his Spirit and sends him not with the promise of success but with the charge to speak faithfully whether or not they listen — the measure of his calling being faithfulness, not results.
1.historical_context: “Son of man” (v.1) — God’s recurring address to Ezekiel (over 90 times) — emphasizes his frail humanity before the divine glory. The Spirit entering to set him on his feet (v.2) shows that prophetic ministry is Spirit-empowered. The commission to a “rebellious house” “whether they hear or refuse” (v.5) defines success as faithful proclamation, not audience response.
1.literary_structure: This call narrative (vv.1–5) follows the throne-vision, the Spirit-empowered raising (v.2) and the commission to the rebellious (vv.3–5) being its core. The refrain “whether they hear or refuse to hear” (v.5, v.7) frames the prophet’s charge.
1.theological_application: God measures his servants by faithfulness, not visible results — speak “whether they hear or refuse to hear” (v.5; 2 Timothy 4:2). Ministry to a rebellious, hardened people is still to be carried out faithfully, for the responsibility is to proclaim, not to guarantee response (Isaiah 6:9–10). The believer is freed from the tyranny of measuring obedience by success, and empowered, as Ezekiel was by the Spirit (v.2), to speak God’s word courageously regardless of reception. God measures his servants by faithfulness, not visible results — speak “whether they hear or refuse to hear” (v.5; 2 Timothy 4:2). Ministry to a rebellious, hardened people is still to be carried out faithfully, for the responsibility is to proclaim, not to guarantee response (Isaiah 6:9–10). The believer is freed from the tyranny of measuring obedience by success, and empowered, as Ezekiel was by the Spirit (v.2), to speak God’s word courageously regardless of reception.
1.christology: “Son of man,” God’s title for the frail prophet (v.1), is taken up by Jesus as his favorite self-designation (Mark 10:45), the Son of Man who is also the glorious figure of Daniel 7:13. The Spirit who empowered Ezekiel (v.2) rested fully on Christ (Luke 4:18) and is given to his witnesses (Acts 1:8). Christ too was sent to a people who largely refused to hear (John 1:11), yet faithfully proclaimed the Father’s word (John 8:28).

6.synthesis: God repeatedly charges Ezekiel, “Be not afraid… nor be dismayed at their looks, for they are a rebellious house” (v.6). A hand stretches out with a scroll, and unrolled it is written “front and back… words of lamentation and mourning and woe” (vv.9–10). The integrative thread is fearlessness grounded in receiving God’s word: against the intimidating hostility of the people (“briers and thorns… scorpions”), God commands the prophet not to fear, and presents him with a scroll full of woe — the message he must internalize and deliver, his courage flowing not from a pleasant task but from the authority of the God-given word.
6.historical_context: The repeated “be not afraid” (v.6) addresses the prophet’s natural dread before a hostile audience compared to briers, thorns, and scorpions. The scroll written “front and back” (v.10) — full to overflowing — with “lamentation and mourning and woe” signals the grievous message Ezekiel must deliver, anticipating the eating of the scroll in chapter 3 (Ezekiel 3:1–3).
6.literary_structure: This section (vv.6–10) joins the charge to fearlessness (v.6) with the presentation of the written scroll (v.9), the scroll of woe being the prophet’s message. It sets up the dramatic eating of the scroll that opens chapter 3.
6.theological_application: Faithful proclamation requires courage that comes from God, not from a favorable reception — “be not afraid of them” (v.6; Jeremiah 1:8). The God-given message is sometimes one of “lamentation and mourning and woe” (v.10), and the messenger must deliver it faithfully rather than soften it to please. The believer learns that courage in witness flows from the authority of God’s word received, freeing us to speak even hard truths without fear of human hostility (Acts 4:29). Faithful proclamation requires courage that comes from God, not from a favorable reception — “be not afraid of them” (v.6; Jeremiah 1:8). The God-given message is sometimes one of “lamentation and mourning and woe” (v.10), and the messenger must deliver it faithfully rather than soften it to please. The believer learns that courage in witness flows from the authority of God’s word received, freeing us to speak even hard truths without fear of human hostility (Acts 4:29).
6.christology: The scroll of woe that Ezekiel must receive and proclaim (v.10) reappears in Revelation, where only the Lamb is worthy to open the scroll of God’s purposes (Revelation 5:1–9), and where John, like Ezekiel, eats a scroll (Revelation 10:9–10). The fearlessness God commands (v.6) is supplied by Christ, who tells his witnesses, “do not fear those who kill the body” (Matthew 10:28), and who himself fearlessly proclaimed both grace and woe (Matthew 23:13).
